content:
AUDIENCE:
The reader is a senior business executive, likely accustomed to high-level strategic thinking rather than technical or scientific details. They have limited or no formal background in science but possess strong analytical and decision-making skills. They value clear, concise, and practical explanations that connect to real-world implications. They are very busy, so they prefer information that is straightforward and to the point, without unnecessary jargon or overly detailed explanations. Anything overly technical or theoretical could confuse or bore them. A confident, respectful tone with simple analogies or relatable language will be most effective.

===BEGIN===
The changing seasons on Earth are caused primarily by the planet’s tilt as it orbits the Sun. Imagine Earth as a spinning top, tilted about 23.5 degrees from the vertical. This tilt means that during different times of the year, either the Northern or Southern Hemisphere leans closer toward the Sun. When your hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, you experience summer—longer days and more direct sunlight. When it tilts away, it’s winter—shorter days and less direct sunlight. This tilt, combined with Earth’s yearly orbit, creates the seasonal cycle. So, seasons are not about how close Earth is to the Sun, but rather about the angle at which sunlight hits different parts of the planet. Understanding this helps explain weather patterns and even impacts business activities linked to climate and energy use throughout the year.
===END===
